I looked into this just now, and do believe that this is a static method after a read of [this comment](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/136097/what-is-the-difference-between-staticmethod-and-classmethod-in-python/1669524#1669524):

> With staticmethods, neither self (the object instance) nor  cls (the class) is implicitly passed as the first argument. They behave like plain functions except that you can call them from an instance or the class. ... Staticmethods are used to group functions which have some logical connection with a class to the class.

Does this make sense to you?

My take would be that json is basically javascript derived and in javascript an array can basically be seen as a has eg
`['this', 'is', 'a', 'list']` can be look at as `{0: 'this', 1: 'is', 2: 'a', 3: 'list'}` so I would unroll them as though they were a hash with a key of their index so `item_0` etc.

In Numpy, it simply means that the array has no elements, while in MXNet, it could mean one of the several following things:
1. the array is empty, and shape is (0,...)
2. the array's shape is completely unknown and needs to be inferred from others in the network
3. the array's shape is partially unknown and needs to be inferred from others in the network

Does it makes sense to return `False` for items 2 and 3?

Again, this is copied from scipy.  I guess I have a slight preference for keeping galsim.LookupTable2D as a drop-in replacement for scipy.interpolate.interp2d, but not a strong one if you think this is sufficiently counter-intuitive.

> Also, I know there is an optimization in the Outer code that saves some of the upperIndex calls, but do you know how much of a time savings that is?

Here's the comparison (including scipy too).  This is for interpolating a 200x200 grid from a 512x512 input grid.

SciPy interp2d
Function linear          took 0.00044 seconds.
GalSim LookupTable2D scatter
Function linear          took 0.00151 seconds.
GalSim LookupTable2D outer
Function linear          took 0.00095 seconds.


So outer is ~50% faster than scatter here.  Still a factor of 2 slower than scipy.  Should note that scipy requires its interpolation argument to be sorted, though, which I didn't want to require since it makes edge wrapping messier.

This part of the code is full of Byriani, not properly documented, and we're not sure about what it does exactly. From there, two strategies are possible when we want to publish a change that impact this code:

- We try to understand how this piece of code work. When we succeed, we adapt the code so that it can work with the a Variable instead of a Column.
    - Advantage: cleaner once its one, _if_ we don't pollute the new `Variable` class with stuff from `Column` that we don't really want to keep.
    - Drawback: high risk of getting lost, and to spend more time migrating this module we don't even know than making the changes we want to do.
- We consider this piece of code as legacy that we are maintaining in a minimalistic way. We try to avoid modifying it. If it needs a column, we give it a column, hoping that one day it will be rebuilt or remove.
    - Advantage: Our time investment is more focused
    - Drawback: Some legacy modules stay in the code

I chose strategy 2, as columns were needed in several part of the code I don't want to deal with.

CLIError as defined in the old core:
```Python 
class CLIError(Exception):
  pass
```

As defined in Knack:
```Python
class CLIError(Exception):
   def __init__(self, message):
     super(CLIError, self).__init__(message)
```

So the reason is because Knack overrides the __init__ and doesn't accept the same arguments.  @derekbekoe is there a reason Knack does this?

```<< i << "\""``` is for, theoretically say that we have a really long string vector like this:

```
[
    "hellowhatsupmarie" : 400,
    "hellowhatsupadam" : 300,
    "hellowhatsuptom" : 200
] 
```
And we truncate everything to "hellowhats" because that's our theoretical cutoff, then:

```
[
    "hellowhats" : 400,
    "hellowhats" : 300,
    "hellowhats" : 200
] 
```

would be our output and technically we are 2x-ing the theoretical string shown to vega, in this instance only "hello..." would be shown in most cases. To differentiate between duplicated string values caused by truncation I added the `<< i << "\"";` to take care of this situation

> Should these both reject, or both capture by value? The prose here doesn't make clear (at least to me) why these are different.

I'll improve the prose.  However, basically, both of these are ones we cannot capture by reference.  We could just default to capturing both by value.  

**However**: it's known ahead of time that capturing @pulumi by value simply will not work (simply because it itself references packages that we won't be uploading).  We could just allow the capturing to seem to succeed (or potentially fail), but the user will most likely then get a cryptic error later on down the line.

The point of erroring early is simply so we can provide good failure path.  In other words, using @pulumi is just going ot fail at this part of our product cycle.  We can either make it fail very cryptically (i.e. errors at runtime, or with suggestions to change how it is imported), or we can detect up front this bad pattern and give a *very good* message about what is going wrong.  

Normally, i'm really not a fan of special casing, or having magic strings.  And i definitely be up for some sort of cleaner mechanism to address this issue.  However, i think this is a very low cost, and *effective* mechanism for quick and clearly helping people avoid something that is almost surely going to otehrwise be a bad experience :)

--

Note: i'm ok with opening a bug to track investigating other things here.  However, i have a real concern that such bugs simply sit around not serving much purpose since they'll almost always (in isolation) be a low priority for us.  I'd rather us just accept this as an acceptable starting point, and we can drive any changes here through new scenarios with clear customers (internal or external) that need those scenarios to work.

The simplest way to install TensorLayer is: 

```bash
# for master version(Recommended)
$ pip install git+https://github.com/zsdonghao/tensorlayer.git 

# for stable version 
$ pip install tensorlayer
```

Dockerfile is supplied to build images, build as usual

```bash
# for CPU version
$ docker build -t tensorlayer:latest .

# for GPU version
$ docker build -t tensorlayer:latest-gpu -f Dockerfile.gpu . 
```
